nodes.name: TV Catchphrases & Quotes
nodes.description: Famous lines and catchphrases from television shows

items.prompt: What animated Fox sitcom features a patriarch whose signature exclamation of frustration was added to the Oxford English Dictionary in 2001?
items.explanation:
Homer Jay Simpson's exasperated "D'oh!" became the character's defining verbal tic
Voiced by Dan Castellaneta, who based the delivery on Jimmy Finlayson's drawn-out "D'ohhh" from Laurel and Hardy films
Added to the Oxford English Dictionary in 2001, defined as an exclamation expressing frustration
Created by Matt Groening; premiered 1989; longest-running American scripted primetime series
Began as shorts on The Tracey Ullman Show (1987) before getting its own series
Springfield's state is intentionally never revealed

items.prompt: The Stark family motto "Winter is coming" serves as an ominous warning throughout which HBO fantasy series based on George R.R. Martin's novels?
items.explanation:
House Stark's words, spoken most often by patriarch Ned Stark (Sean Bean) in Season 1
HBO, 2011–2019, 8 seasons, 73 episodes; based on Martin's A Song of Ice and Fire novels
The phrase functions as both a literal weather forecast and a metaphor for looming existential threats
Became a ubiquitous cultural meme applied to any approaching difficulty or deadline
The show held the record for most Emmy Awards won by a drama series (59 total)
Filmed across Northern Ireland, Croatia, Iceland, Spain, and Morocco

items.prompt: A high school chemistry teacher turned methamphetamine manufacturer declares "I am the one who knocks" to his terrified wife in which AMC drama?
items.explanation:
Spoken by Walter White (Bryan Cranston) to his wife Skyler in Season 4, Episode 6 ("Cornered")
The line marks Walter's full transformation from mild-mannered teacher to ruthless drug lord Heisenberg
AMC, 2008–2013, 5 seasons, 62 episodes; created by Vince Gilligan
Cranston won four Primetime Emmy Awards for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series
Set and filmed in Albuquerque, New Mexico
Spawned the prequel series Better Call Saul (2015–2022), also critically acclaimed

items.prompt: Which NBC sitcom (1994–2004) about six Manhattan twenty-somethings features a womanizing Italian-American actor whose signature pickup line became a cultural phenomenon?
items.explanation:
Joey Tribbiani's "How you doin'?" delivered by Matt LeBlanc as a flirtatious greeting
Joey is a struggling actor who lands a role on the fictional soap opera Days of Our Lives
NBC, 1994–2004, 10 seasons, 236 episodes; set in Manhattan's Greenwich Village
The six core cast members negotiated collectively and each earned $1 million per episode by the final season
Filmed at Warner Bros. Studios in Burbank, not New York City
The Reunion special on HBO Max (2021) drew massive viewership 17 years after the finale

items.prompt: Regional manager Michael Scott repeatedly delivers the innuendo-laden retort "That's what she said" in which NBC mockumentary sitcom set at a paper company in Scranton, Pennsylvania?
items.explanation:
Michael Scott (Steve Carell) uses the double-entendre punchline as his signature joke throughout the series
Adapted from the British phrase "Said the actress to the bishop," used by Ricky Gervais's David Brent in the UK original
NBC, 2005–2013, 9 seasons, 201 episodes; adapted from the BBC series created by Gervais and Stephen Merchant
Carell departed after Season 7; the show continued two more seasons without him
Pioneered the single-camera mockumentary format for American sitcoms alongside Parks and Recreation
The joke predates the show—Wayne's World on Saturday Night Live popularized the phrase in the 1990s

items.prompt: A theoretical physicist with an eidetic memory shouts "Bazinga!" after executing a prank or sarcastic remark in which CBS sitcom about Caltech scientists?
items.explanation:
Sheldon Cooper's catchphrase, played by Jim Parsons; first used in the Season 2 finale ("The Monopolar Expedition")
Created behind the scenes by writer Stephen Engel, who would say "Bazinga!" after telling bad jokes in the writers' room
CBS, 2007–2019, 12 seasons, 279 episodes; created by Chuck Lorre and Bill Prady
Parsons won four Primetime Emmy Awards for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Comedy Series
Warner Bros. trademarked the word "Bazinga" in 2012
The show ended because Parsons chose not to renew his contract, not because of cancellation

items.prompt: George Costanza's girlfriend uses the filler phrase "yada yada yada" to skip over inconvenient details in which NBC sitcom famously described as "a show about nothing"?
items.explanation:
From Season 8, Episode 19 ("The Yada Yada," aired April 24, 1997); George discovers his girlfriend uses the phrase to gloss over shoplifting
Created by Larry David and Jerry Seinfeld; NBC, 1989–1998, 9 seasons, 180 episodes
The episode was written by Peter Mehlman and Jill Franklyn, earning an Emmy nomination for Outstanding Writing
The phrase existed before the show but became universally popularized by this episode
Also popularized "double-dipping," "sponge-worthy," "close talker," and "man hands"
Final episode drew 76 million viewers; 30-second ads reportedly cost $2 million

items.prompt: The Vulcan greeting "Live long and prosper," accompanied by a split-fingered hand gesture inspired by a Jewish priestly blessing, originated on which 1960s NBC science fiction series?
items.explanation:
First spoken by Spock (Leonard Nimoy) in the Season 2 premiere "Amok Time" (1967); scripted by Theodore Sturgeon
The accompanying Vulcan salute was devised by Nimoy, based on the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) he saw as a child at an Orthodox synagogue
The traditional reply is "Peace and long life"
NBC, 1966–1969, 3 seasons, 79 episodes; created by Gene Roddenberry
Cancelled after three seasons due to low ratings, then became a massive phenomenon through syndication
Spawned 13 feature films and multiple spinoff series spanning six decades

items.prompt: Host Jeff Probst extinguishes a contestant's torch and declares "The tribe has spoken" at each elimination ceremony on which CBS reality competition that premiered in 2000?
items.explanation:
Spoken by Probst at every Tribal Council after the votes are read and a contestant is eliminated
The phrase was coined accidentally by producer Mark Burnett in conversation two days before filming the first season in Borneo
CBS, premiered May 31, 2000; 48+ seasons as of 2025; filmed in locations from Borneo to Fiji
The first season finale drew 51.7 million viewers, making it a cultural phenomenon
Ranked #35 on TV Land's 100 Greatest TV Quotes and Catch Phrases (2006)
Considered the "Adam and Eve" of reality-show elimination catchphrases, inspiring countless imitators

items.prompt: Incompetent magician G.O.B. Bluth repeatedly mutters "I've made a huge mistake" after his schemes backfire in which Fox/Netflix comedy about a wealthy dysfunctional family?
items.explanation:
G.O.B. Bluth (Will Arnett) delivers the line after virtually every failed scheme, though other Bluth family members also say it
Fox, 2003–2006 (3 seasons); revived by Netflix in 2013 and 2018–2019 (2 more seasons)
Created by Mitchell Hurwitz; won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series in 2004
Cancelled by Fox despite critical acclaim due to low ratings; became a cult hit through DVD sales
The show's layered callbacks, recurring gags, and foreshadowing were ahead of their time for sitcom storytelling
Will Arnett's G.O.B. is a professional magician (he insists on "illusionist") who performs under the stage name "The Amazing G.O.B."

items.prompt: Womanizing corporate lawyer Barney Stinson commands his friends to "Suit up!" before nights out in which CBS sitcom (2005–2014) narrated in flashback by Bob Saget?
items.explanation:
Barney Stinson (Neil Patrick Harris) wears a suit at all times and insists his friends do the same
First uttered in the pilot episode; Harris has said the phrase originated from Bob Saget in 1991, then was adopted for the show
CBS, 2005–2014, 9 seasons, 208 episodes; created by Craig Thomas and Carter Bays
Barney's other catchphrases include "Legen—wait for it—dary!" and "Challenge accepted"
Harris won multiple awards for the role despite the character being a departure from his earlier clean-cut Doogie Howser image
The divisive series finale remains one of the most debated endings in sitcom history

items.prompt: Coach Eric Taylor rallies the Dillon Panthers with "Clear eyes, full hearts, can't lose" before every football game in which NBC/DirecTV drama set in small-town Texas?
items.explanation:
Spoken by Coach Eric Taylor (Kyle Chandler) as a pre-game rallying cry, first used in the pilot episode
NBC/DirecTV, 2006–2011, 5 seasons, 76 episodes; based on H.G. Bissinger's 1990 nonfiction book and the 2004 film
Chandler won the Primetime Emmy for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series in 2011
Set in the fictional town of Dillon, Texas; filmed in Austin, Texas
Barack Obama adopted the phrase during his 2012 re-election campaign, prompting the show's creator Peter Berg to ask him to stop
The show was saved from cancellation by a co-production deal with DirecTV's 101 Network after Season 2

items.prompt: Stan and Kyle exclaim "Oh my God, they killed Kenny! You bastards!" after their hooded friend dies in nearly every episode of which Comedy Central animated series set in a small Colorado town?
items.explanation:
Kenny McCormick dies in almost every episode of the early seasons, only to reappear alive in the next
The line originated in the 1992 animated short The Spirit of Christmas: Jesus vs. Frosty, which predates the series
Comedy Central, 1997–present; created by Trey Parker and Matt Stone, who voice most characters
Parker and Stone have said the "You bastards!" part is directed at themselves as writers for killing the character
Ranked #35 on TV Land's 100 Greatest TV Quotes and Catch Phrases
Kenny's muffled voice (he speaks through his parka hood) is Parker mumbling into his hand

items.prompt: Announcer Johnny Olson first invited audience members to "Come on down!" as contestants on which CBS daytime game show, the longest-running in U.S. television history?
items.explanation:
Johnny Olson coined the phrase at the show's 1972 premiere; his wife estimated he said it 42,000 times before his death in 1985
CBS, premiered September 4, 1972; over 10,000 episodes aired; longest-running game show in U.S. history
Bob Barker hosted from 1972 until his retirement in 2007; Drew Carey has hosted since
Olson's successors Rod Roddy, Rich Fields, and George Gray all continued the tradition
Contestants are called from the studio audience to guess the retail prices of merchandise
Barker famously ended each episode with "Help control the pet population—have your pets spayed or neutered"

items.prompt: Host Regis Philbin built suspense by asking contestants "Is that your final answer?" on which ABC prime-time game show that became a cultural phenomenon in 1999?
items.answer: Who Wants to Be a Millionaire
alternates: Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?
items.explanation:
Regis Philbin's tension-building confirmation became inescapable in American pop culture during the show's peak
ABC, premiered August 16, 1999; adapted from the British original hosted by Chris Tarrant (1998)
At its peak in 1999–2000, the show aired multiple nights per week and dominated U.S. ratings
Lifelines included "Phone a Friend," "Ask the Audience," and "50:50"
The first U.S. million-dollar winner was John Carpenter (November 19, 1999), who used his Phone a Friend to tell his father he was about to win
Ranked on TV Land's 100 Greatest TV Quotes and Catch Phrases (2006)
items.cardBack: Who Wants to Be a Millionaire
